text: Current modern globalization, the global power structure, and development policies all continue to adhere to the principle of economic growth. Criticism of GDP and the social and ecological side effects of growth is increasingly prevalent. Current growth is always associated with the use of large amounts of energy and resources, which threaten our ecological systems. In response to the limitations of growth, post-growth theories have been developed that give an idea of the alternatives. In this article three alternative approaches are explained: steady-state economics, degrowth and the well-being economy. They will be discussed by means of an analysis of their origins, developments and influence on policy-making, in order to be able to highlight their differences and possible opportunities for cooperation and thus to further the global debate about how to dismantle the prevailing growth paradigm and create an economy that is sustainable for people and the planet.
